text: The surgeon uses a vessel sealer device in the right hand to dissect and divide the fat surrounding the stomach, moving towards the distal aspect where the pylorus is located. The stomach is lifted with the left hand to create a window into the lesser sac, providing clear visualization of the posterior space behind the stomach.

The surgeon navigates up the greater curvature of the stomach, with the diaphragm visible above and the heart beating through it. A liver retractor is positioned for optimal visualization. The phrenic artery and vein are seen near the vessel sealer, supplying the diaphragm. As the spleen comes into view, the surgeon carefully dissects around the area where the esophagus meets the stomach, operating within a centimeter of the spleen to avoid injury.

The surgeon continues to mobilize the posterior aspect of the stomach, carefully dissecting flimsy adhesions from the surrounding fat, spleen, and diaphragm. The fundus of the stomach is being targeted for resection, which is crucial in the sleeve gastrectomy procedure.

The surgeon works next to the diaphragm, examining the area where the esophagus meets the stomach for signs of a hiatal hernia. The diaphragmatic crura are visible, ensuring the stomach remains within the abdominal cavity. The surgeon carefully manipulates the stomach near the pylorus, preserving this portion as it will remain with the patient. A stapler is visible, and a 36 French bougie is passed to gauge the sleeve size for consistency in the gastrectomy procedure.

The bougie is passed by the anesthesiologist at the head of the patient, while Dr. Harnish controls the stapler. The first stapler fire uses a black load, 60 millimeters in length, to transect the stomach. The stapler is then reloaded for the second fire, which uses a purple load, following the bougie within the body of the stomach. The surgeon carefully avoids narrowing at the incisura, a critical area during sleeve gastrectomy.

The surgeon reloads the stapler with a purple, sixty millimeter reinforced staple load and continues stapling up the body of the stomach. The pancreas is visible posterior to the stomach, distinguishable from the surrounding fat, and is carefully avoided during the procedure. The final staple fires remove the fundus of the stomach, a crucial step for reducing hormone secretion related to appetite.

The surgeon uses a 45-millimeter stapler to complete the transection of the stomach, removing approximately 85% of the stomach over a 36 French bougie. The divided portion of the stomach is extracted from the patient, concluding the operation.
